featuredSetups.label: Vector sum view
featuredSetups.description: Open a clean addition case with the head-to-tail guide visible.

setup.note: Use this to read a complex number as both a point and a vector.

featuredSetups.label: Rotate by i
featuredSetups.description: Open the multiplication case where the multiplier acts like a quarter-turn.

setup.note: Use this to see multiplication as rotation and scaling.

pageIntro.definition: Read complex numbers as points and vectors on one plane, then keep addition and multiplication geometric instead of symbolic-only.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: A complex number z = a + bi can be read as the point (a, b) or as the vector from the origin to that point.

v2.intuition: Complex numbers get much easier when the algebra and the picture never split apart: the same z is both a point and an arrow on one plane.
v2.whyItMatters: This turns complex arithmetic into something you can see and reason about, which makes later rotation, trigonometry, and polar-form ideas much less abstract.

equationSnapshot.note: Keep the plane picture in mind: one formula tells you where the point is, and the other tells you how multiplication turns and scales it.

guidedSteps.title: Read z and w on the plane
guidedSteps.goal: See that a complex number is already both geometry and algebra before you start combining anything.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start in Vector sum, keep the addition graph open, and move only the w sliders for a first pass.
guidedSteps.notice: The ordered pair and the arrow move together. You are not switching between two different objects.
guidedSteps.explain: z = a + bi means the point (a, b) and the vector from the origin to that same point.

guidedSteps.title: Build the sum
guidedSteps.goal: Use the same plane to read z + w as both component addition and head-to-tail geometry.
guidedSteps.doThis: Keep addition view on and use the head-to-tail guide to read where z + w lands.
guidedSteps.notice: The sum endpoint matches both the translated second arrow and the component-by-component addition rule.
guidedSteps.explain: Complex addition behaves like vector addition because the real parts add on one axis and the imaginary parts add on the other.

guidedSteps.title: Turn multiplication into motion
guidedSteps.goal: See multiplication as a turn-plus-scale action instead of just a component rule.
guidedSteps.doThis: Switch on Multiplication view and apply Rotate by i before you change w yourself.
guidedSteps.notice: A multiplier near the unit circle can turn z a lot without stretching it much.
guidedSteps.explain: For products, magnitudes multiply and arguments add, so w changes both the size and direction of z.

guidedSteps.title: Check the plane story
guidedSteps.goal: Test whether the geometric and algebraic descriptions still agree when you explain the idea back to yourself.
guidedSteps.doThis: Use the quick checks, then build one multiplication case that turns the result without stretching it much.
guidedSteps.notice: The right answer should keep saying the same thing: the plane picture and the algebra are describing the same object.
guidedSteps.explain: If you can explain a point, a vector, a sum, and a product on the same plane, the concept is starting to hold together.

learned: A complex number can be read as the point (a, b) and as the vector from the origin to that point.
Addition on the complex plane is the same head-to-tail story as vector addition.
Multiplication changes size and direction together because magnitudes multiply and arguments add.
wrapUp.misconception: Complex multiplication is not only a symbolic component rule. The geometry is the clearest way to read what the product is doing.

nextConcepts.title: Unit Circle / Sine and Cosine from Rotation
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Keep the same turn-on-a-plane picture and reuse it for trig coordinates.

nextConcepts.title: Polar Coordinates / Radius and Angle
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Carry the same distance-and-direction reading into radius-angle coordinates.

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Stay on the plane and try your own add-versus-multiply cases until you can predict the turn and stretch before you switch the view.

paragraphs: Complex numbers become easier to trust when the algebra and geometry stay on the same plane. This bench keeps z, w, and the current result visible together so a + bi reads as both an ordered pair and a directed arrow.
Addition should feel like vector addition, and multiplication should feel like one point being rotated and scaled by another complex number.

keyIdeas: A complex number z = a + bi can be read as the point (a, b) or as the vector from the origin to that point.
The magnitude |z| is the distance from the origin, and the argument tells the direction of the same point.
Complex multiplication combines scaling and rotation on the same plane.

commonMisconception.myth: Complex multiplication is just a symbolic component rule, so the geometric picture is optional.
correction: The component rule is real, but the geometry is what makes the result readable.
On this plane, multiplying by w changes both the size and the direction of z.

workedExamples.title: Live worked example
workedExamples.intro: Use the current plane state rather than a detached worksheet.

items.title: Read the current vector sum
items.prompt: For the current addition view, where does z + w land on the plane?

steps.label: 1. Write the current points
steps.template: The current points are z = {{zValue}} and w = {{wValue}}.

steps.label: 2. Add the real and imaginary parts separately
steps.template: The sum keeps the plane honest: ({{realPartValue}} + {{operandRealValue}}, {{imaginaryPartValue}} + {{operandImaginaryValue}}).

steps.label: 3. Read the new endpoint
steps.template: That gives z + w = {{sumValue}}, so the sum lands at ({{sumRealValue}}, {{sumImaginaryValue}}).

applyAction.label: Apply the vector-sum view
applyAction.observationHint: The head-to-tail guide keeps the component addition and the geometry tied together.

items.title: Read a multiplication rotation
items.prompt: In multiplication view, what scale and turn does w apply to z, and where does z · w land?

steps.label: 1. Read the multiplier
steps.template: The current multiplier is w = {{wValue}}, so its size |w| = {{scaleFactorValue}} and its turn is about {{rotationDegValue}}°.

steps.label: 2. Apply scale and turn to z
steps.template: Start from z = {{zValue}}. Multiplication scales z by {{scaleFactorValue}} and adds about {{rotationDegValue}}° to its direction.

steps.label: 3. Read the product endpoint
steps.template: The live product is z · w = {{productValue}}, landing at ({{productRealValue}}, {{productImaginaryValue}}) on the same plane.

applyAction.label: Apply the rotation view
applyAction.observationHint: The rotation cue and unit circle show that multiplication can turn z first, while the multiplier magnitude controls how much the product stretches.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a multiplication case where the product lands in the second quadrant without making the point much longer than the original z.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the multiplier needs to act mostly like a rotation, mostly like a stretch, or both before you try it.
miniChallenge.answer: You need a multiplier whose argument turns z into the second quadrant while its magnitude stays near one.
miniChallenge.explanation: The product direction comes from the added argument, while the size comes from the multiplier magnitude.

equations.label: Complex-coordinate form
equations.meaning: Reads one complex number as a point on the plane with real part a and imaginary part b.

equations.label: Magnitude and argument
equations.meaning: Turns the same point into a distance from the origin and a direction on the plane.

equations.label: Geometric multiplication rule
equations.meaning: Explains why multiplying by w scales and rotates z.

variableLinks.label: Real part of z
variableLinks.description: Moves z left or right on the real axis.

variableLinks.label: Imaginary part of z
variableLinks.description: Moves z up or down on the imaginary axis.

variableLinks.label: Real part of w
variableLinks.description: Changes where the second complex point sits on the real axis.

variableLinks.label: Imaginary part of w
variableLinks.description: Changes the vertical component and therefore the direction of w.

variableLinks.label: Multiplication view
variableLinks.description: Switches the result from a head-to-tail sum to the geometric product.

presets.label: Rotate by i
presets.description: A multiplier with magnitude 1 and argument 90 degrees.

presets.label: Canceling sum
presets.description: An addition case where the two arrows partly cancel.

overlays.label: Head-to-tail guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show the translated second vector used in the sum.
whatToNotice: The sum still uses the same two component pairs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps complex addition tied to vector addition.

overlays.label: Unit circle cue
overlays.shortDescription: Keep a one-unit reference circle visible.
whatToNotice: A multiplier with magnitude 1 can still rotate the point strongly.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the size story of multiplication easier to read.

overlays.label: Rotation cue
overlays.shortDescription: Show the turn from arg(z) to arg(z · w).
whatToNotice: Multiplication changes direction by adding angles.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the argument-addition rule into a visible motion.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the plane, the result arrow, and the active graph in view together.

items.text: In addition view, the result arrow can be read both as component addition and as a head-to-tail sum on the same plane.
A multiplier near the unit circle can turn z a lot without stretching it much.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict the geometric change before you switch the preset or the view.

items.prompt: If w = i, what should happen to z in multiplication view?

choices.label: z should rotate about 90 degrees while keeping the same magnitude.
z should always double in length.
z should collapse to the origin.

items.explanation: The multiplier i has magnitude 1 and argument 90 degrees, so it acts like a quarter-turn without scaling the point.
items.observationHint: Turn the rotation cue on and compare the original direction with the product direction.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live plane picture, not from a detached rule.

questions.prompt: What is the most honest way to read z = a + bi on this bench?

choices.label: As both the point (a, b) and the vector from the origin to that point.
Only as a symbolic expression with no geometric meaning.
Only as two unrelated real numbers.

questions.explanation: The whole point of the complex plane is that the algebra and geometry describe the same object.

questions.prompt: If |w| is close to 1 but arg(w) is large, what is the cleanest expectation for z · w?

choices.label: The product should rotate a lot without changing size very much.
The product should always become much longer.

questions.explanation: A multiplier near the unit circle mostly changes direction, not size.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the plane honestly: build the right scale-and-turn case instead of guessing from formulas alone.

items.title: Rotate onto the positive imaginary axis
items.prompt: Build a multiplication case where z · w lands almost on the positive imaginary axis while the multiplier magnitude stays close to one.
items.successMessage: You built a genuine rotation-first multiplication case. The product landed near the positive imaginary axis because the argument changed strongly while the scale factor stayed near one.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a complex plane with z, w, and the current result arrow. Sliders or dragging change the real and imaginary parts of both points, and a toggle switches between addition view and multiplication view.
One graph shows the real and imaginary parts of z + w as the real part of w changes. A second graph shows the real and imaginary parts of z · w under the same sweep.

seo.title: Complex Numbers on the Plane
seo.description: Explore complex numbers as points and vectors, then read addition and multiplication geometrically on a live complex plane.
